2024 Annual Report

  • 2024 In Review
  • Grant Highlights
  • People
  • Investments

Message From the CEO

As we reflect on the accomplishments of the Murdock Trust throughout 2024, our 49th year of giving, I am filled with gratitude and pride for the remarkable progress we have made in our mission to serve the Pacific Northwest. As always, our work is motivated by the evergreen donor intent of Jack Murdock and our desire to pursue human flourishing for the common good through capacity-building investments that are innovative and sustainable. We remain inspired by the passionate and faithful service of individuals and organizations across our region doing impactful work.  

As 2024 began, we embraced the theme of renewal, focusing on reflection and the opportunities it brings for growth and improvement. This mindset guided our efforts as we introduced exciting updates to our work, including the full realization of our new sectoral model. This model, rooted in the evergreen donor intent of our benefactor Jack Murdock, has allowed us to better align our strategies with the evolving needs of our community. 

We marked the culmination of over a year of dedicated work with the launch of our new Grants Management System (GMS). This system, designed to streamline the application process and enhance our ability to capture and utilize data, represents a significant investment in our capacity to support the nonprofit community more effectively. By leveraging technology, we gave time back to ourselves and those we serve for strategic work, unleashed greater human potential, and improved our grantmaking process. 

Throughout the year, we achieved several key milestones, including the official publication of our sectoral strategies. These advancements have refined our approach and priorities, ensuring greater clarity for the nonprofits we support and amplifying the impact of our investments.  

Like many of you, we’ve done this while navigating the shifting, sometimes perplexing, and often complex external landscape. Our focus has been on responding with curiosity, courage, thoughtfulness, and a deep focus on finding a third way that leads to highest common denominator solutions. While there is much I could note here, let me simply say that we remained committed to the social sector, committed to those we serve, and committed to working for the common good with those with whom we agree and disagree.  

Looking ahead, we are excited to continue building on the foundation we have established, fostering collaboration, and driving positive change. The end of 2024 led us into our 50th Anniversary celebration (explore more here), which has only renewed my deep gratitude for the hard work and dedication of our team, the support of our Trustees, and the invaluable contributions of our community partners. Together, we are making a meaningful impact and ensuring a brighter future for all. 

In gratitude,

A Look Back at 2024

$89m

Total Granted

320

Grants Awarded

1290

Program Participants

$15.2m to Artistic & Cultural Expression

$34.1m to Civic Engagement & Community Services

$7.0m to Education & Leadership Development

$21.9m to Health & Environmental Stewardship

$10.2m to Scientific Research

What Does the Murdock Trust Do?

Watch the trailer below (and full video at this link) to learn more about the Murdock Trust’s vision for partnering with nonprofits – the backbone of thriving communities and the heart of lasting change. 

Nonprofits at Work

In the midst of significant increases in inquiries and grant applications, as well as organizational restructure at the Trust, we remained committed to processing as many applications as possible in 2024. The nonprofits and projects described below represent a small sampling of the good work done across our region last year, and they are truly the “Why” behind the Murdock Trust’s “What.” We invite you to explore, be inspired, and learn more about these nonprofits. View full list of strategic grants here >

Interactive Nonprofits Gallery
Filters

Click/Tap to Learn More

Hold & Drag to See More

Chugachmiut

Since 1974, Chugachmiut has served as a Tribal consortium dedicated to advancing health, promoting equity, and supporting self-determination among the seven Tribes of Alaska’s Chugach region. In 2024, with support from the Trust, Chugachmiut took a major step forward in constructing a new, Tribal-owned and operated health clinic. Designed as a regional hub, this clinic will offer comprehensive medical, dental, and behavioral health services to communities spread across a vast and remote landscape—spanning 5,000 miles of coastline, over 50 islands, and more than 20,000 square miles of mountains.

Nordic Ski Club of Fairbanks

The Nordic Ski Club of Fairbanks fosters a vibrant, year-round skiing culture by maintaining world-class trails, hosting inclusive events, and nurturing athletes of all ages and abilities. Through education, volunteerism, and community-driven programs, the club strengthens connections and promotes healthy outdoor living in Alaska’s Interior. In 2024, the Trust was able to support the hiring of the Club’s first full-time executive director, a step in the organization’s growth that is anticipated to increase sustainability and community collaborations.

Alaska Christian College

Alaska Christian College empowers Alaska Native and rural students through faith-based higher education, personal mentoring, and academic support that prepares them for leadership and service. By fostering strong cultural identity and offering resources for success, the college strengthens both individual lives and the broader Alaskan community. In 2024, the Trust supported campus improvements around the counseling center and athletic center, strengthening students’ ability to engage in campus life safely and comfortably.

Fairbanks Children’s Museum

The Fairbanks Children’s Museum sparks curiosity and creativity through hands-on exhibits and playful learning experiences for children of all ages. By offering inclusive programs, sensory-friendly events, and educational outreach, the museum provides access to enriching opportunities for every family who visits. Support from the Trust for a new development director is anticipated to enhance fundraising sustainability and strengthen community partnerships with schools, peer organizations, and businesses, so that children can engage in the process of creative discovery for years to come.

Sitka Conservation Society

Sitka Conservation Society (SCS) works to protect the natural environment of the Tongass National Forest while supporting the development of economically, socially, and environmentally sustainable communities within Southeast Alaska. In 2024, the Trust had the chance to partner with SCS on final renovations for their retreat property, Sea Pony Farm, which provides a collaborative space for leaders to find respite, convene, and work together with the intention of developing sustainable communities – while being meaningfully connected to the land they are on. 

Log Cabin Literary Center

Log Cabin Literary Center (the Cabin), nestled in a historic log cabin in Boise, Idaho, is the state's only nonprofit dedicated entirely to fostering a love of reading and writing. Through programs like Writers in the Schools, summer writing camps, readings, lectures, and author-led events, it serves over 8,000 individuals annually, nurturing creativity and community across generations. In 2024, the Trust was able to support the Cabin’s facility renovations, which will add classroom and office space, improve accessibility, and add an outdoor event space so that more readers, writers, and learners throughout the Treasure Valley can be welcomed into their programming. 

St. Vincent de Paul of Southwest Idaho

St. Vincent de Paul of Southwest Idaho is a faith-based nonprofit working to prevent homelessness by offering personalized support to individuals and families in need. Through food pantries, thrift stores, reentry services, and housing assistance, the nonprofit empowers neighbors across the Treasure Valley to achieve stability and self-sufficiency. With funding for two new staff positions in its Reentry Career Development Program, the organization will expand its impact by providing career-readiness training and transitional support to individuals moving from incarceration to meaningful employment. 

The Nature Conservancy in Idaho

The Nature Conservancy in Idaho partners with Indigenous communities, businesses, governments, other nonprofits, and individuals to help protect Idaho’s biologically important habitats and restore the condition and function of its rivers, wetlands, forests, and grasslands. Over more than five decades, it has helped protect more than 450,000 acres of land and 200 miles of rivers and streams statewide. Acquiring a conservation easement along the Pahsimeroi River with Trust funding will help restore salmon levels, which positively impacts local ecosystems and economies. 

Idaho Community Foundation

Idaho Community Foundation (ICF) unleashes generosity and supports philanthropy through establishing and managing charitable funds for Idahoans who want to enrich the quality of life throughout the state. As ICF seeks to engage new Idahoans and increase its impact, the nonprofit sought Trust funding for two new positions in advancement and philanthropic advising that will advance these goals and create new pathways for flourishing across Idaho.

University of Idaho

University of Idaho (UI) is the flagship research institution in Idaho with a strong history of pursuing cutting-edge scientific research. UI researchers at the College of Natural Resources work in Idaho and globally to enhance the conservation and sustainability of fisheries, wildlife, forests, rangelands, and water resources. Support from the Trust for new walk-in and ultracold freezers will enable cryopreservation, which is the freezing of biological materials at low temperatures and which will support ongoing analysis that is critical for research and student training.

Helena Food Share

Helena Food Share (HFS) provides food to neighbors in a respectful and dignified way and partners with its community to eliminate hunger in the greater Helena area. In 2023 alone, HFS provided more than 8,000 individuals from nearly 4,000 households with food assistance services such as food pantry outlets, packs for students, emergency food packs distributed via first responders and social service agencies, and more. Through support from the Trust, HFS will build a new centralized facility, including storage, a retail food pantry, and administrative offices.  

ExplorationWorks

ExplorationWorks is an interactive science center in the heart of Helena that aims to ignite a lifelong love of science to enrich Montana’s future. Through STEM exhibits, engaging experiences, educational programs, and summer camps, ExplorationWorks sparks curiosity and fosters learning in visitors of all ages. Facility renovations will convert a balcony into a much-needed classroom and exhibit space to meet the community’s growing need for science education, serving an additional 8,000 children and youth each year.  

Blackfoot Challenge

The Blackfoot Challenge is a globally recognized leader in cooperative conservation and is based in western Montana’s Blackfoot Watershed. Its mission is to coordinate efforts to conserve and enhance natural resources and the rural way of life in the Blackfoot Watershed for present and future generations, and it has done this through permanently protecting 1.3 million acres of land from development, creating a 5,600-acre, community-owned and managed forest, restoring native trout habitat, and more. Trust funding will support new research staff to continue informing these critical conservation efforts. 

North Valley Music School

North Valley Music School (NVMS) enriches Montanan communities through music education, appreciation, and performance by providing musical opportunities for all ages. As the only nonprofit community music school in Montana, it provides private music education, group classes, summer camps, a children’s choir, chamber ensembles, open mic acoustical jams, and low-cost music instruction for veterans. No interested student is turned away for inability to pay. In 2024, the Trust supported the construction of a new facility that will enhance accessibility, energy efficiency, and capacity to serve 25% more students annually.

Weissman Hood Institute

The Weissman Hood Institute (formerly known as McLaughlin Research Institute) in Great Falls, Montana, advances integrated biomedical and rural health research with a focus on diseases that disproportionately affect rural communities. By combining basic, translational, and clinical research—including groundbreaking neurogenetic studies on Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, and other degenerative diseases—the Institute actively involves the local community in scientific discovery. Trust funding will support the purchase of a small animal MRI, helping to drive research forward and reduce the urban-rural gap in biomedical research infrastructure. 

La Clinica

La Clinica aims to serve people in Jackson County who are most in need of healthcare services through exceptional, affordable, and compassionate care. They do this through eight neighborhood health centers, 19 clinics at public schools, a student health and wellness center at Southern Oregon University, an outreach clinic at a shelter and resource center for people without housing, a learning center, and a mobile health center. The expansion of La Clinica’s Acute Care Clinic with support from the Trust will increase access to urgent care services for those in Jackson County and help contain health care costs by reducing unnecessary emergency department use.

Compassion First

Compassion First is a Christian relief and development organization with a mission to provide end-to-end personalized care for each girl rescued from sex trafficking in Indonesia. It takes a holistic approach, investing in prevention efforts, survivor care, and government collaboration. In 2024, the Trust supported new staffing to invest in Compassion First’s partnership with church communities to maximize their impact and expand their donor pool. 

OMSI

The Oregon Museum of Science and Industry (OMSI) inspires curiosity through engaging science learning experiences, fostering experimentation and the exchange of ideas, and stimulating informed action. Trust funding will support the conversion of OMSI’s 20,000 square foot Natural Sciences Hall into an immersive, interdisciplinary space for visitors to explore the impacts and challenges around climate change, as well as solutions and local efforts to address those challenges.

Bag & Baggage Productions

Bag & Baggage Productions (B&B) is a professional theatre company that produces and presents theatrical productions, film screenings, lectures, fundraisers, and other events out of its historic facility in Hillsboro, OR. Through highly collaborative partnerships with local businesses and nonprofits, B&B boosts the economy and cultural vibrancy of downtown Hillsboro. In 2024, the Trust supported the growth of the Native Theater Project which will provide intergenerational opportunities for Native people to tell their own stories, a project founded in response to the underrepresentation of Native stories in the entertainment industry, especially those told by Native people themselves. Photo courtesty of Bag & Baggage Productions.

The Oregon FFA

The Oregon FFA Association is a nonprofit organization dedicated to developing the potential of young Oregonians for premier leadership, personal growth, and career success through agricultural education. With more than 15,000 members across 118 chapters, it offers a range of programs including leadership development, career exploration, and community service. In 2024, the Trust supported the hiring of a Local Teacher Success Specialist who is intended to drive retention of agriculture teachers across the state.

Linfield University

Linfield University is a private liberal arts institution in McMinnville, OR, with additional campuses in Portland and online offerings. The university is ranked as the top liberal arts college in Oregon for social mobility and among the best in the nation for supporting students from lower-income backgrounds to achieve success. The Trust supported start-up costs for a new faculty member with expertise in biochemistry, filling an important curricular and research expertise gap. This grant was part of the Trust’s Research Start-Up Program for New Science Faculty.  

STEM Paths Innovation Network

Stem Paths Innovation Network (SPIN) creates STEM opportunities for communities of color through high-quality programming that engages families, uplifts communities, and empowers students. Specifically supporting those in South King County, where STEM jobs, training, and education are sorely lacking and where the region’s most diverse communities live, SPIN connects elementary students with STEM professionals, offers after-school and summer enrichment programs, equips high school students to pursue STEM businesses, and more. Trust funding will support the hiring of a development director, enabling SPIN to increase partnerships and drive overall growth to engage more community members in their critical work. 

Empowering Youth & Families Outreach

Empowering Youth & Families Outreach (EYFO) is a nonprofit organization based in Seattle, Washington, dedicated to fostering resilience and leadership among underserved youth, particularly youth of color. Founded in 2001, EYFO offers a range of programs—including after-school tutoring, summer camps, college and career readiness initiatives, and family support services—to empower students and families to thrive in the face of systemic challenges. The Trust had the opportunity in 2024 to support capital costs for a new youth enrichment center to expand outreach and programs and increase impact. 

The University of Puget Sound

The University of Puget Sound is a private liberal arts college located in Tacoma, WA, founded in 1888. It offers a wide range of undergraduate programs and select graduate degrees, emphasizing experiential learning and community engagement. In 2024, the Trust supported the hiring of new faculty in the biology department, specifically in the area of developmental biology and genetics, filling recent gaps and a strategic research focus in the emerging field of bioinformatics. 

University of Washington

The University of Washington (UW), founded in 1861, is a premier public research university located in Seattle, WA. The Washington Nanofabrication Facility at the university has national and international recognition in pursuing cutting-edge research in the field of fabricating micro- and nano-scale materials and devices. Trust funding supported the purchase of major instrumentation that will ignite new and more efficient research at the Nanofabrication Facility, serving academic, government, and industry researchers. 

Museum of Northwest Art

The Museum of Northwest Art collects, preserves, interprets, and exhibits art created in the Pacific Northwest, supports artists, and strives to integrate art into the lives of all people. Located in the small waterfront town of La Conner, WA, the museum is free and open to all, with educational programming igniting impact throughout the year. New curator staff, hired with support from the Trust, will increase collections stewardship and amplify the museum’s unique identity in the Pacific Northwest. 

Genetic Support Foundation

The Genetic Support Foundation (GSF), based in Olympia, WA, is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing accessible, independent genetic counseling services to individuals and families, particularly focusing on underserved communities across the Pacific Northwest. Through telehealth platforms and partnerships with healthcare providers, GSF aims to empower patients with objective genetic information, fostering informed health decisions and promoting equity in healthcare. Trust support for a new development director will increase the sustainability of GSF’s funding plan so they can provide this critical support to community members long into the future.

National Forest Foundation

The National Forest Foundation (NFF) is the nonprofit partner of the U.S. Forest Service that works to engage Americans in community-based programs to foster collaborative stewardship and leverage investment in support of the National Forest System. Trust funding for new staff will support an initiative in western Washington focused on conducting large-scale watershed restoration activities on three National Forests: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie, Olympic, and Gifford Pinchot. This project aims to restore more fully functioning, climate-resilient watersheds that improve water quality, provide habitat for species recovery, and support long-term carbon management. 

Moving Picture Institute

Moving Picture Institute (MPI) is a modern-media production house and talent incubator that creates and supports high-impact film and video content designed to entertain, inspire, and educate audiences with captivating stories about human freedom. MPI’s films reach hundreds of millions of people through theatres and digital streaming platforms. Trust funding will support MPI’s Documentary Storytelling Workshop and Production Lab, increasing the number of filmmakers in MPI’s network and expanding the reach of the high-quality films they create.

Trinity Forum

The Trinity Forum (the Forum) is a faith-based nonprofit that cultivates thoughtful Christian leadership by engaging leaders with the best of literature and public theology to explore life’s biggest questions. Through curated readings, events, and discussions, it fosters spiritual and cultural renewal in both personal and public life. Trust funding will support the modernization of the Forum’s website and a CRM upgrade to better serve constituents engaging with the Forum’s content online. 

Programming

I have had the distinct honor of leading the Murdock College Science Research (MCSR) Conference and seeing the impact of the cohorts and conferences listed below over the years. This has given me insight into the caliber of eager participants who attend our programs, ready to invest in their own growth for the sake of their nonprofits, disciplines, institutions, and communities. It has also given me a chance to witness the strength of our Murdock Trust team and strategic partners who put on these programs with great heart, expertise, and dedication. Cheers to all who made 2024 an impactful year of programming!

Cohort-Style Programs

Board Leadership Faith-Based

In 2024, 93 participants from 16 faith-based nonprofits deepened their board leadership skills through personalized coaching and hands-on training.

Board Leadership & Development

In 2024, 76 participants from 14 nonprofits engaged in training and cohort-based coaching for more effective board leadership.

Essentials of Development

93 professionals from 19 organizations sharpened their fundraising expertise in 2024 through in-person and virtual sessions and personalized coaching. 

Green Room: Board Leadership for Leaders of Color

30 leaders of color from our region participated in a year-long cohort, preparing to either step into board leadership for the first time or to strengthen their existing board commitments. 

Peer Cohorts: Excelling Beyond the Beginning

13 executive directors in their third to sixth year of leadership joined a collaborative, two-year cohort in 2024, diving deep into shared leadership challenges and opportunities. 

Conferences

Murdock College Science Research Conference

Under the theme “Saving Our Planet Through Scientific Research,” 332 undergraduate students and 90 faculty gathered for undergraduate research presentations, networking, and a lecture. 

Partners in Science Research Symposium

Around 200 secondary school science teachers from across the nation gathered in San Diego for several days of research presentations, collaborative learning, and networking. 

Partners in Science Teaching Practices Conference

25 secondary school science teachers gathered in Vancouver, WA, to discuss best practices and methodologies to bring to their classrooms. 

Vision & Call Retreat

172 interns supported by the Murdock Trust Vision & Call program enjoyed fellowship, learning, and dedicated time to consider vocation at this annual retreat. 

Women in Leadership Conference

90 women in nonprofit mid-level leadership gathered for career learning, networking, and workshops to support professional and personal growth. 

Changing Faces in 2024

Press Enter or Space to view biography. Press Escape to close.

After more than 13 dedicated years at the Trust, Conner Peckham transitioned out of his role as Business Systems Analyst to pursue other opportunities in philanthropy. 

After four years at the Murdock Trust, including three years on the Investments team, Shannon Hendricks left the Trust to pursue another role in the nonprofit space. 

May LaFlamme joined our Communications team as our very first Digital Media Specialist, elevating our digital presence and helping amplify stories of community collaborations across the region.

Celebrating Conner Peckham

After more than 13 years at the Murdock Trust, Conner Peckham stepped into new opportunities in the philanthropic space in 2024. Starting as Administrative Assistant in 2010, Conner steadily took on roles supporting communications, grants, programs, and technology, ultimately serving as Business Systems Analyst. He was instrumental in modernizing Trust operations, bringing clarity and care to every system he touched. Just as impactful, Conner’s kindness and creativity shaped team traditions and culture in ways we still cherish today. 

“Conner was a stable and strong contributor throughout his 13.5 years investing in the Murdock Trust and the well-being of grantees. He is a man of utmost integrity and character and was a strong culture builder within the organization. Thank you for all that you gave us, Conner.” - Mike True, Chief Financial Officer, Murdock Trust 

Total Assets, Grants Approved & Grants Paid

In 2024, we approved more than $90 million in new grants and processed grant payments of more than $115 million in commitments. While these numbers will fluctuate year over year – dependent on demand, capacity, and economic factors – the steadfast work behind each project these dollars support is what continues to inspire our team. Hover over the graph below to learn more about the Trust’s history of giving.

Hover over the graph for more information. (Amounts in thousands. Example: $1,140,574 = $1,140,574,000)

Investments

In 2024, the Murdock Trust continued to employ a highly relational investment strategy that navigated the competing demands of any given moment with a long-term outlook. This past year saw several enduring themes emerge that we wanted to get ahead of and we have been in the process of intentionally shifting some allocations accordingly. At every step, we worked with trusted managers as we stewarded the resources entrusted to us in Jack Murdock’s will for the benefit of the common good.

The Murdock Trust divides all potential investments into either low, medium, or high “risk/reward buckets” and considers all types of investment products — without preconceived ideas or built-in biases — for all three categories. Our investment objectives follow directly from this “three-bucket” allocation strategy. More information on this strategy ›

Investment Types

Low Risk/Return
Cash & Alternative Cash
Fixed Income Relative Value
Long U.S. Treasuries
Medium Risk/Return
Public Equity
Private Debt & Mezzanine
Real Estate & Infrastructure
Other Mid-Risk/Return Strategies
High Risk/Return
Private Equity
Distressed Debt
Venture Capital
Growth Equity

About Our Benefactor

Our benefactor, Melvin J. “Jack” Murdock, was an avid learner, innovator, and entrepreneur with business interests throughout the Pacific Northwest. At a young age, he established his own business selling and servicing radios and electrical appliances, and from there he began working with Howard Vollum. The two would go on to develop the oscilloscope and co-found Tektronix, which would become a major Oregon employer and contribute significantly to the tech growth of the Pacific Northwest. Upon his untimely death in 1971, his will directed three Trustees to establish a charitable trust that would support thriving communities in the region he lived in and loved.

Learn About Jack › View Jack’s Timeline ›

Our Mission

To build capacity in nonprofits driving innovative and sustainable outcomes in the Pacific Northwest.

Our Vision and Values ›
